module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
sublecture:
2.1.3 Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844 - Part 3
, Incomplete unit
content: So now we come to the second part where we continue our discussion of Marxist economic and philosophical manuscripts. So in the first part we discussed Marxist's idea of alienation, estrangement and the first form of alienation which is alienation from the product. So what does that mean? That, you know, I as a worker, whatever the product is created at the end in the factory or in whatever form, it doesn't really belong to me. I cannot just take it and go and sell it. And a less visible form, a more subtle form of the same alienation from the product is that the product no longer has my stamp. It is not a creative expression of who I am. So that's what we discussed in the first part of the economic and philosophical manuscript. Now we'll discuss the other three forms of alienation that Marx discusses and then we'll end with how does this apply to us and how can we think about the relevance of this in our current day scenario? So the second form of alienation that Marx talks about is alienation from the activity of production itself. So it's not just the end object that is created it is also while I'm creating that product, am I alienated? The act of producing itself. Mark says that in the modern capitalist economy, that act of producing itself is an alienating act. So work becomes a process of a estrangement while you are working. So Marx's, Marxist quotation which I will read now where he says" In her work she does not affirm herself but denies herself. Does not feel content but unhappy does not develop freely her physical and mental energy, but modifies her body and ruins her mind. The worker therefore only feels himself outside his work and in his work feels outside himself. He feels at home when he's not working and when he's working he does not feel at home. His labour is therefore not voluntary but coerced. It is forced labour. It is therefore not the satisfaction of a need. It is nearly a means to satisfy needs external to it". So a lot of us can resonate with this perhaps. And you know, going back to the Charlie Chaplin video which you must have watched initially you can see how Charlie Chaplin you know, exemplifies the worker and when he finally gets a break from his routine repetitive activity, he goes to the bathroom and that's when he just exhales. He goes, ah. So finally it is during the break that he can be himself not during his work hours. Now for Marx, this is really twisting the human nature because for Marx for us to be human is to reflect ourselves through our work. That is one of the crucial aspects of us. It's an a sense of us as humans to express ourselves, to create something and to embody an object with who we are. And so for him, he goes on to say that as a result, therefore workers only feel themselves freely active in their animal functions, eating, drinking, procreating or utmost in their dwelling and in dressing up. And in his human functions that worker no longer feels himself to be anything but an animal. What is animal becomes human and what is human becomes animal. So this is a very evocative quote from Marx, and let me repeat just the last part so you can remember it " What is animal becomes human and what is human becomes animal". So we feel human only in our, you know, eating and procreating and going home and resting. But what is deeply human? The essence of humans which is work. We feel like animals. We just want to do it just to survive. It is a means of sustenance. Nothing more. So Marx clarifies further that eating, drinking, dressing up are also human functions separated. But if they are separated from the sphere of productive human activity and they are the sole ultimate ends, then they can be considered as purely animal functions. What according to Marx, Marx calls this species being. So according to Marx, free conscious activity is human beings, species character free conscious activity. Unlike animals, humans produce even when there is no physical need for themselves, they produce universally. This is the species being of humans according to Marx, our ability to produce for universal use, not just for us to survive, but whatever we produce can be used by many. Now this is the essence of humans according to Marx. Now, keep in mind here again, the video of the social media influencer. Again, just to clarify, I'm not endorsing her but just you know trying to find an example so that it's easy for you to understand the contrast. So why are her videos so popular even beyond China? Because she, she's expressing one of these deeply human earnings, in my opinion to be close to nature. Use the gifts of nature to produce these beautiful clothes, these beautiful food from scratch. And so we resonate with that. We connect with that this need to embody in our work, our true inherent spirit. And of course, if you look at social media, I'm sure you'll find thousands of other similar examples. Now what Marx is saying is that in the modern capitalist system, humans are torn from the species being which is their real objective as a member of the human species. So in conclusion, what is Marx saying in the economic and philosophical manuscript? To summarize, Marx is saying that the modern capitalist system alienates humans. One from the product of their labour that is capital. Second, from the activity of labour, the process itself becomes a process of estrangement. Third, from species being the essence of what it means to be human. And fourth, \, from other humans whom we now confront only as other commodities with whom we must continuously compete to get work. So this fourth point, Marx speaks more about it. Where he says he asks us this question, if the product of my labour doesn't belong to me, then who does it belong to? And Marx asks, does it belong to God? And Marx goes on to explain that in times past human beings did believe that the product of their labour belonged to God. So, you know, your farmers, you have the best produce you go and offer it to God or to nature. So but Marx says the modern life, the more humans have subjected nature to their own ends, the more miracles of the God were rendered superfluous by the miracles of industry. So it's clearly not that Gods are nature who are enjoying the products of our labour. So then who is enjoying the products of our labour? It's not God. It's not nature. Then who is it? Mark says, well, yes, it is other humans who are the masters of the products of labour. So through estranged alienated labour, the worker creates the relation to the capitalist who is the master now of the product of our labour. So according to Marx, private properties does the product the result, the necessary consequence of alienated labour. Just as the Gods are originally not the cause but the effect of man's intellectual confusion. So through estranged, alienated labour, the worker creates the relationship to the capitalist who is the master of the product of labour. And so what Marx is saying is that it is the private property is as the product, the result, the necessary consequence of alienated labour. It's alienated labour which is creating private property, not the other way around. So now here Marx gives an analogy and says just as the Gods are originally not the cause but the effect of man's intellectual confusion. So here we see that Marx is taking a pot shot, not just at the religious people but also at the political economist. So he started by taking pot shots at him, at political economists, and he is ending also by taking pot shots at both political economists and religious people. And he's claiming that private property is the product of alienated labour. So alienated labour for Marx is the source of the issue. And so for him, increasing wages by law, let's say there's a law that says that everyone should have a minimum wage but for Marx, this is not a solution. So for him, he says increasing wages by law is therefore nothing but better payment for the slave. And would not win either for the worker or for labour, their human status and dignity. So having a minimum uniform, minimum wage that's not a solution for Marx, for, for him that's just better payment for the slave. Even if we have complete equality of labour where everyone let's say gets paid exactly the same lowliest worker to the highest paid scientists, everybody, let's say by law is paid exactly the same. According to Marx, that still does not change the basic relationship of alienation For him, that is the problem. So all it does is by saying that everyone has to be paid the same. It just turns society into an abstract capitalist who provides all humans with work and the means of subsistence. After we finish discussing the manifesto, we'll come back to this and we'll see how you know, how communism finally was played out. Did it really match what Marx conceptualized initially? You know, one could argue that it does not. So I will ask you to recall these arguments of Marx again, when we are discussing the manifesto. So what is Marx saying? Marx is saying worker emancipation. That is changing this basic fact of alienation of labour. This is what will lead to universal human emancipation and this, this entire argument, it basically lays the foundation for Marxist's later critique of capitalism and his push for communism as a solution. Now that part is Marx as an ideologist. And you know, I would like us to clarify that you know, our purpose here is not to really debate about communism versus capitalism because that's, at the end of the day, it's a more political, ideological discussion. We are more interested in Marx as a social theorist, so we will discuss alienation and then we will discuss the manifesto from that perspective. So what are the key takeaways that from the economic and political manuscripts that you and I as a social theorist should take away? One is how Marx puts together his argument. You know, keep in mind that Marx is really going against established system, and how is he creating an argument to convince you and me that he is actually getting to the core of the issue. So he starts with a fact what he says is a fact that workers are getting poorer, the more they produce workers become commodities while wealth and power concentrate elsewhere. And then alienation separates humans from their products, from the activity of their labour, from their species being and from each other. So this is what he really starts with. And then he goes on to describe in his manifesto what he thinks is the solution to all of these problems which we will discuss in the next subsection. But before we go there, I would like us to take a step back and think about which of these ideas are relevant today. You know, the first idea the worker becomes ever poorer. I, I, you can see some posts from social media which I just sort of randomly pulled up. I'm sure there are many, many other such posts. This is about a woman 51 years old who is working 12 hour shifts at Amazon, one of the biggest companies today. She's working at this warehouse in NYC. She sleeps in her car, so she's homeless. Showers at Planet Fitness. Charges her phone at Starbucks, and we are talking about one of the richest economies and societies in the world today. And this is an example of the worker becoming poorer. What about India? Does this apply to us? Another post which I pulled up about the plight of food delivery workers within India, you know, and show pictures of food delivery workers going on their scooter in scorching heat and rain. Yet another example from India, a recent book by Manu Joseph is titled, Why The Poor Don't Kill Us: The Psychology of Indians. Which discusses why attacks on the rich, poor, the by the poor are rare in India despite persistent inequality. So you see the thing he, he he is, he's asking why we have peace, you know, we should have lack of peace. So the question is not why do we have violence? The question is, why do we have peace given the extent of inequality that we have? So it's a thought provoking book. Again, the question is not debating agreement or disagreement. The question is thinking about inequality and thinking about whether these issues that Marx has raised? Marx raised so many years back is that still relevant to us? So research by many of you might be familiar, research by Piketty, Thomas Piketty, and others shows the negative impact of inequality on everybody, not just those in the lower strata. For example, inequality can dampen growth. It is correlated with worse health and educational outcomes for everyone, not just the poor but even for the rich lower levels of trust, increased polarization. So all of these are impacts of inequality which show us that the original questions that Marx raised even if we perhaps don't agree with his solution, those original questions about alienation, estrangement are still relevant. So I invite you to think about what are the ideas from Marx that resonated with you. And think about how do you, how would you respond? Forget about Marx for the moment. Forget about Adam Smith. Forget about all that. And think about if these issues resonate with you, how do you think about it? What do you think is the solution?
